%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.3-2 The Exponentially Varying Sinusoid

> **Section Objective:** Learn to sketch a sinusoid whose amplitude shrinks over time by splitting the signal into two parts and combining them using envelopes.

Consider the signal \(x(t) = 4e^{-2t}\cos(6t - 60°)\). This is not a pure cosine — its amplitude is not constant. It is a cosine whose size is controlled by a decaying exponential. Many students can read the formula but freeze when asked to draw it quickly on an exam.

This section gives you a reliable three-step method: read the exponential part, read the cosine part, then combine them using upper and lower envelopes. Once you separate the two factors, the sketch becomes straightforward. The general form is \(x(t) = Ae^{-at}\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\), and every signal of this type follows the same sketching logic.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Three-step sketching method: draw the exponential envelope, draw the shifted cosine, then combine them. The final waveform lives inside the decaying bounds.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215399148-4337.png)

$$x(t) = Ae^{-at}\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)$$
*This formula describes a cosine wave whose amplitude at every instant is controlled by the exponential factor \(Ae^{-at}\), so the oscillation grows or shrinks over time rather than staying at a fixed height.*

## 1. Read the Signal as Two Separate Parts

Every exponentially varying sinusoid has exactly two factors working together:

- **Envelope factor:** \(Ae^{-at}\) — sets the amplitude bound at each moment in time.
- **Oscillating factor:** \(\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\) — provides the oscillation shape.

### WORKED EXAMPLE

For \(x(t) = 4e^{-2t}\cos(6t - 60°)\):

**Exponential part:** \(4e^{-2t}\) is a decaying exponential with initial value \(4\) and time constant \(\tau = 1/a = 1/2 = 0.5\) s. This means the amplitude drops by a factor of \(e\) every \(0.5\) s.

**Cosine part:** \(\cos(6t - 60°)\) has angular frequency \(\omega_0 = 6\) rad/s, giving period \(T_0 = 2\pi/6 \approx 1\) s. The \(-60°\) phase means the cosine is delayed to the right by approximately \(60°/360° \times T_0 \approx 1/6\) s.

### EXAM TIP

Do not try to sketch the product directly. Always read decay speed, oscillation period, and phase shift as three separate quantities first. This prevents the most common exam mistake: drawing a cosine with the wrong starting point or wrong peak heights.

$$\tau = \frac{1}{a}, \qquad T_0 = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$$
*These two formulas give the decay speed \(\tau\) and the oscillation period \(T_0\) — the first two quantities you should read from the signal before attempting any sketch.*

## 2. Build the Sketch Using Envelopes

Once you have read the two parts separately, the combination step is straightforward. The final waveform is a cosine whose peak heights are constrained at every instant by the upper envelope \(+Ae^{-at}\) and the lower envelope \(-Ae^{-at}\).

### AMPLITUDE TABLE FOR \(4e^{-2t}\)

| Time \(t\) (s) | Envelope value \(4e^{-2t}\) |
|---|---|
| \(0\) | \(4.00\) |
| \(0.5\) | \(4/e \approx 1.47\) |
| \(1.0\) | \(4/e^2 \approx 0.54\) |
| \(1.5\) | \(4/e^3 \approx 0.20\) |
| \(2.0\) | \(4/e^4 \approx 0.07\) |

### SKETCHING PROCEDURE

1. Draw the upper envelope \(+4e^{-2t}\) as a dashed decaying curve.
2. Draw the lower envelope \(-4e^{-2t}\) as its mirror image below the axis.
3. Place the phase-shifted cosine \(\cos(6t - 60°)\) inside these bounds, starting slightly to the right of \(t = 0\) due to the phase delay.

The waveform touches \(+4e^{-2t}\) exactly at positive peaks and touches \(-4e^{-2t}\) exactly at negative peaks.

#### Quick Check

Ask yourself: if the envelope gets smaller, what happens to the peak heights? They must shrink by exactly the same factor — the cosine can never exceed its envelope.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="exam_pattern_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 The oscillating waveform is always bounded by \(+4e^{-2t}\) above and \(-4e^{-2t}\) below. Positive peaks touch the upper envelope; negative peaks touch the lower envelope.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777215598969-3781.png)

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Split the signal into an exponential envelope \(Ae^{-at}\) and an oscillating cosine \(\cos(\omega_0 t + \theta)\) — never sketch the product directly.
- Read three parameters first: time constant \(\tau = 1/a\), period \(T_0 = 2\pi/\omega_0\), and phase delay \(\theta/\omega_0\).
- Draw envelopes \(\pm Ae^{-at}\) first, then fit the phase-shifted cosine inside — peaks must touch the envelopes.

*In the next section we will continue using visual signal features to sketch and interpret waveforms more confidently.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
